heroOverview: Tyrosinemia refers to a group of rare inherited disorders in which one of the enzymes responsible for breaking down the amino acid tyrosine is deficient, causing toxic metabolites to accumulate and damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system. The most severe form, type I, affects approximately 1 in 100,000 to 120,000 live births worldwide and, without treatment, can cause acute liver failure in infancy or hepatocellular carcinoma in childhood. A pharmacological breakthrough occurred with nitisinone (NTBC), introduced in the early 1990s, which blocks the production of toxic metabolites upstream and has transformed the prognosis from near-certain early death to survival into adulthood with near-normal liver function in most patients started on therapy early. All three forms are autosomal recessive and are diagnosable through newborn screening programmes, making early identification and treatment possible before symptoms begin.

keyStats.prevalence: Type I (most severe): approximately 1 in 100,000–120,000 live births globally; higher in Quebec, Canada (1 in 16,000) due to a founder effect; types II and III are rarer
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; autosomal recessive inheritance; higher prevalence in populations with founder effects including French-Canadians, Scandinavians, and some Middle Eastern communities
keyStats.avgAge: Type I typically presents in the first months of life; type II may present in infancy to childhood with ocular and skin manifestations; type III usually presents in childhood with neurological symptoms
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 400–1,000 new cases diagnosed annually worldwide; cumulative prevalence is several thousand patients receiving nitisinone therapy

definition: Tyrosinemia (ICD-10: E70.21) encompasses a set of inborn errors of tyrosine catabolism caused by enzyme deficiencies at different steps of the degradation pathway. Tyrosine, derived from dietary protein and from hydroxylation of phenylalanine, is normally catabolised through a series of enzymatic steps to fumarate and acetoacetate. Type I tyrosinemia (hepatorenal tyrosinemia) results from deficiency of fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (FAH), the final enzyme; the resulting accumulation of fumarylacetoacetate and its derivatives is acutely hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic and is also a potent mutagen that drives hepatocellular carcinoma. Type II (oculocutaneous tyrosinemia, Richner-Hanhart syndrome) results from deficiency of hepatic tyrosine aminotransferase and causes painful corneal crystals and palmoplantar keratoderma through peripheral tyrosine deposition. Type III results from deficiency of 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase and manifests primarily as neurological dysfunction. Transient tyrosinemia of the newborn is a separate, non-genetic, self-limiting condition seen in premature infants receiving high protein intake. All three classic types are autosomal recessive and are diagnosable through elevated plasma tyrosine on tandem mass spectrometry newborn screening. The condition falls under metabolic and endocrine medicine with involvement from hepatology, ophthalmology, and neurology depending on the type.

typesClassification.description: Caused by fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (FAH) deficiency. The most severe form, presenting with acute liver failure or a more chronic liver disease with renal tubular dysfunction (renal Fanconi syndrome), peripheral neuropathy, and very high risk of hepatocellular carcinoma if untreated. Responds dramatically to nitisinone.

Caused by hepatic tyrosine aminotransferase deficiency. Features painful corneal tyrosine crystal deposits, photophobia, and hyperkeratotic skin plaques on the palms and soles. Liver and kidney involvement is absent or minimal. Managed with low-tyrosine and low-phenylalanine diet.
Caused by 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase deficiency. Manifests as intellectual disability, ataxia, and seizures in childhood. Very rare with fewer than 30 cases reported worldwide.

A non-genetic, self-limiting form caused by immature hepatic enzyme activity in premature or term newborns receiving high protein intake. Resolves spontaneously within weeks and does not cause lasting harm. Included here to clarify the distinction from inherited tyrosinemia.

primarySymptoms: Acute or progressive liver dysfunction in infancy — manifesting as jaundice, coagulopathy, ascites, and hepatomegaly in type I, which can rapidly progress to acute liver failure.
Elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST, bilirubin) and prolonged prothrombin time detected on routine or targeted blood tests, often the first laboratory finding.
Renal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome) in type I — presenting with aminoaciduria, glycosuria, phosphaturia, and metabolic acidosis, leading to poor growth and rickets-like bone disease.

Neurological crises in type I — acute episodes resembling porphyria, with severe limb pain, vomiting, hypertonia, paralysis, and sometimes respiratory failure, triggered by accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid.
Growth failure and failure to thrive in infancy, reflecting combined hepatic, renal, and nutritional compromise in severe type I.
Painful eye symptoms in type II — photophobia, lacrimation, and red eyes caused by corneal tyrosine crystal deposits, often the first presenting feature in this type.
Palmoplantar hyperkeratosis in type II — thickened, painful, plaque-like skin on the palms and soles from subepidermal tyrosine deposits.
Intellectual disability, ataxia, and seizures in type III, often presenting gradually through childhood.
Odour resembling boiled cabbage in some untreated type I patients, from accumulating methionine metabolites alongside tyrosine.
Hepatocellular carcinoma developing in childhood or young adulthood in inadequately treated or undetected type I — the most feared long-term complication.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained jaundice or prolonged coagulation in the first weeks of life, beyond what is expected for physiological jaundice.
Elevated plasma tyrosine or succinylacetone on newborn screening — the latter is pathognomonic for type I and triggers immediate evaluation.
Poor weight gain or recurrent vomiting in an infant that does not respond to dietary adjustments.
Recurrent unexplained painful limb crises in a child with liver disease, suggesting a neurological crisis from delta-aminolevulinic acid accumulation in type I.
emergencySigns: Acute liver failure with jaundice, coagulopathy, and encephalopathy in a young infant — a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital admission and urgent metabolic specialist review.
Neurological crisis with acute severe limb pain, vomiting, hypertonia, and progressive paralysis — can progress to respiratory failure requiring ventilation.
Hypoglycaemia with altered consciousness in an infant with known or suspected liver disease.
Sepsis in a child with type I tyrosinemia and known immunocompromise from liver dysfunction.
Sudden abdominal pain with a rapid increase in liver size — may indicate hepatic haemorrhage from an underlying hepatic tumour.

causes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function mutations in the FAH gene on chromosome 15q25 cause complete or near-complete absence of the final enzyme in the tyrosine catabolism pathway. Accumulating fumarylacetoacetate alkylates cellular DNA and proteins, producing hepatocellular necrosis and mutagenesis that drives liver cancer.

Biallelic mutations in the TAT gene reduce conversion of tyrosine in the liver, raising plasma and tissue tyrosine levels. In the cornea and skin, tyrosine precipitates as crystals that trigger a local inflammatory response, producing the characteristic eye and skin manifestations.

Mutations in HPD impair the second step of tyrosine degradation. Accumulating 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid and related metabolites interfere with neurotransmitter synthesis pathways and cause the neurological phenotype.

All three classical types require biallelic mutations — one from each parent. Carrier frequency for type I is approximately 1 in 150–200 in the general population and considerably higher in populations with founder effects such as French-Canadians from the Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean region, where carrier frequency approaches 1 in 22.

A single ancestral FAH mutation (IVS12+5G>A splice-site variant) accounts for over 90% of alleles in French-Canadian patients from Quebec, explaining the 1 in 16,000 disease prevalence in that region compared with 1 in 100,000 globally.

riskFactors.description: Having a sibling or parent who is a known carrier significantly raises the a priori probability; siblings of an affected child have a 25% risk of being affected and a 50% risk of being a carrier.
The founder effect in this Quebec region gives a carrier frequency of approximately 1 in 22, making this the highest-risk population globally; antenatal screening is standard in this region.
First or second cousin unions substantially increase the probability of both parents carrying the same recessive mutation, raising the risk of an affected offspring by the degree of shared ancestry.
A distinct founder mutation contributes to modestly elevated carrier frequency in Scandinavian populations compared with the general European background rate.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of newborn screening in birth country
riskFactors.description: Countries without expanded newborn screening programmes will not detect tyrosinemia before symptoms appear, delaying treatment by weeks to months and substantially worsening prognosis.

riskFactors.factor: Missed or delayed newborn screen result
riskFactors.description: Even in countries with screening, laboratory processing delays or failure to follow up abnormal results can allow liver damage to occur before nitisinone and dietary therapy begin.

affectedDemographics: Infants of any ethnic background with autosomal recessive family history
French-Canadian children, especially those from the Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean region of Quebec
Children with Scandinavian, Finnish, or Middle Eastern ancestry with elevated carrier frequency
Male and female infants equally — no sex predilection
Premature infants may have transient neonatal tyrosinemia (a distinct, benign entity)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is most commonly made through newborn bloodspot screening, which measures plasma succinylacetone by tandem mass spectrometry. Succinylacetone is pathognomonic for type I tyrosinemia and is produced only when FAH is deficient; its detection on the newborn screen triggers immediate referral to a metabolic specialist and confirmatory testing. Plasma amino acid analysis shows elevated tyrosine in all three types, but tyrosine elevation alone is non-specific; succinylacetone in urine or plasma provides specificity for type I. In countries without newborn screening or when screening is missed, type I tyrosinemia typically presents with acute liver failure in the first weeks to months of life, and the diagnosis is confirmed by urine organic acid analysis revealing succinylacetone alongside elevated plasma tyrosine and methionine. Molecular genetic testing of the FAH gene identifies the specific mutations and is important for family counselling and pre-natal diagnosis. For type II, the diagnosis is suspected when a child presents with corneal crystals and palmoplantar keratoderma alongside elevated plasma tyrosine; slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist documents the characteristic subepithelial tyrosine deposits. Liver biopsy is rarely needed for diagnosis and carries bleeding risk; it may be performed later in type I patients to evaluate fibrosis or detect early hepatocellular carcinoma. Alpha-fetoprotein is monitored serially in type I patients as a tumour marker, with levels that fall on nitisinone therapy but should be tracked every three to six months lifelong.

diagnosticTests.test: Newborn bloodspot screening (succinylacetone by tandem mass spectrometry)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary screening test for type I tyrosinemia; succinylacetone is pathognomonic and detects affected infants before symptom onset, enabling immediate treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A few drops of blood are taken by heel-prick in the newborn nursery at 24–72 hours of life and blotted onto a filter paper card. Results take two to five business days and are sent to the family's clinician.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms elevated plasma tyrosine in all three forms; also detects elevated methionine in type I, which accompanies liver dysfunction and serves as a severity marker.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting venous blood sample is sent to a specialist metabolic laboratory; results take one to three business days. Timing relative to feeds matters in neonates.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine succinylacetone and organic acid analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms type I by demonstrating succinylacetone in urine; also shows delta-aminolevulinic acid elevation that explains neurological crises.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh urine sample collected in a plain container, usually during a clinical visit; analysis is performed in a specialist laboratory within two to five days.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the degree of hepatic damage; prolonged prothrombin time is often the most sensitive early indicator of liver dysfunction in infants with type I.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood sample; results available within hours in an acute setting or the next day in outpatient monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tumour marker monitored serially in type I patients; AFP is typically markedly elevated at diagnosis, falls with nitisinone treatment, and rising levels despite treatment prompt imaging to exclude hepatocellular carcinoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood test; part of the standard monitoring protocol every three to six months for life in type I.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hepatic nodules, fibrosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma; MRI is preferred for its superior soft-tissue characterisation without radiation, especially in paediatric patients monitored lifelong.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound takes 15–20 minutes and is painless; MRI takes 30–60 minutes and younger children may require sedation or general anaesthesia. Performed every six to twelve months in type I.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific pathogenic variants for family counselling, carrier testing of siblings and parents, and pre-natal diagnosis in subsequent pregnancies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory; results take two to four weeks.

treatmentOverview: The treatment of type I tyrosinemia was transformed by the introduction of nitisinone (NTBC) in the early 1990s. Nitisinone inhibits 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase, the enzyme two steps upstream of FAH, thereby blocking the formation of toxic fumarylacetoacetate before it can accumulate. This upstream blockade prevents hepatic necrosis, eliminates neurological crises, and dramatically reduces — though does not eliminate — hepatocellular carcinoma risk. Treatment with nitisinone should be started as soon as the diagnosis is confirmed, ideally within the first days of life in screen-detected infants. Nitisinone is always combined with a low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet because the drug raises plasma tyrosine by blocking its degradation; without dietary restriction, accumulating tyrosine produces the corneal and skin complications of type II. Dietary management targets plasma tyrosine below 400–500 micromoles per litre. Patients receive regular monitoring of plasma tyrosine, succinylacetone, alpha-fetoprotein, liver function, and liver imaging. Liver transplantation was the only curative option before nitisinone and is now reserved for patients who present with acute liver failure before the drug can take effect, who fail medical management, or who develop hepatocellular carcinoma not amenable to surveillance and localised resection. For type II, the mainstay of treatment is a low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet, which resolves corneal crystals within weeks and improves skin lesions over months; nitisinone is not indicated. Ophthalmological follow-up ensures corneal healing. Gene therapy approaches for type I are under active clinical investigation.

medicalTreatments.name: Nitisinone (NTBC)
medicalTreatments.description: An orphan drug that inhibits 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase, blocking the generation of toxic fumarylacetoacetate and maleylacetoacetate in type I. Taken orally once or twice daily at a dose of approximately 0.5–2 mg/kg/day, titrated to keep urine and plasma succinylacetone undetectable. It is taken for life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Transforms survival: 10-year survival on nitisinone exceeds 90% compared with less than 10% without treatment; reduces hepatocellular carcinoma risk from near-certain to approximately 10% in patients started in the first six months of life.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet
medicalTreatments.description: Essential in all three types. In type I and II, restricts dietary tyrosine (from protein) and phenylalanine (which is converted to tyrosine) using specialised amino acid formula that provides adequate protein without the aromatic amino acids. Natural protein is introduced in small, carefully measured amounts. Targets plasma tyrosine of 250–500 micromoles per litre.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In type II, dietary restriction alone resolves corneal crystals within 3–6 weeks in approximately 80–90% of patients; combined with nitisinone in type I, dietary restriction prevents ocular complications in virtually all cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D and phosphate supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Type I patients with renal tubular dysfunction develop phosphate wasting, hypophosphataemia, and vitamin D-resistant rickets before metabolic control is established. Oral phosphate and vitamin D supplements correct the bone disease and should be continued until renal function normalises on nitisinone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises phosphate and vitamin D levels in approximately 70–80% of patients; radiological healing of rickets occurs within 3–6 months of combined metabolic and supplement therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Fresh frozen plasma and vitamin K for coagulopathy
medicalTreatments.description: In acute hepatic presentations of type I before nitisinone takes effect, coagulopathy is treated with fresh frozen plasma, vitamin K, and sometimes cryoprecipitate to bridge the patient until metabolic control is achieved.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Temporarily corrects coagulation parameters in over 85% of acute presentations; definitive correction requires restoration of hepatic synthesis capacity with nitisinone or transplantation.

medicalTreatments.name: Liver transplantation
medicalTreatments.description: Curative for the hepatic manifestations of type I and was the standard of care before nitisinone; now reserved for acute liver failure at presentation, medical treatment failure, or hepatocellular carcinoma. The transplanted liver has normal FAH activity but residual renal mutations remain, so post-transplant patients still need low-tyrosine diet monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Five-year survival after liver transplantation for tyrosinemia exceeds 90% in experienced centres; eliminates hepatocellular carcinoma risk from the liver but does not reverse pre-existing renal disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Replaces the deficient FAH enzyme with a functioning donor liver. Indicated for acute liver failure before nitisinone can act, failure of medical management, or the development of hepatocellular carcinoma.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year survival above 90% in experienced paediatric transplant centres; hepatic complications do not recur in the transplanted organ.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gene therapy (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Viral vector delivery of a functional FAH gene to hepatocytes has shown promise in mouse models and is entering early-phase human clinical trials; not yet standard of care but may offer a future cure without transplantation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary protein management and amino acid formula
alternativeTreatments.description: Alongside prescribed dietary restriction, some centres use novel amino acid mixtures to optimise branched-chain and other amino acid nutrition while maintaining strict aromatic amino acid limits, improving growth outcomes.

whySeeSpecialist: Tyrosinemia requires lifelong management by a multidisciplinary metabolic team including a metabolic physician, dietitian specialised in inborn errors of metabolism, hepatologist, and ophthalmologist. Nitisinone dosing, dietary tyrosine targets, succinylacetone monitoring, and AFP surveillance require specialist expertise to optimise. Patients should be managed at or in close liaison with a centre with experience in inherited metabolic disorders; in the UK, these are specialist metabolic centres within NHS England; in the US, they are typically academic medical centres with inborn errors of metabolism programmes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a paediatrician or internist specialised in inherited metabolic disorders (biochemical genetics) affiliated with a centre that manages at least several tyrosinemia patients per year. The ideal centre has an in-house specialist metabolic dietitian, access to rapid plasma amino acid and succinylacetone measurement, a paediatric hepatologist, and a liver transplant programme on site or by formal referral pathway. Ask specifically about the centre's protocol for AFP surveillance and their threshold for imaging and biopsy in the setting of rising AFP.
preventionStrategies: Universal newborn bloodspot screening with succinylacetone as a tier-one analyte enables pre-symptomatic diagnosis in countries with expanded programmes.
Genetic counselling and carrier testing for siblings and parents of an affected child, enabling pre-natal diagnosis in subsequent pregnancies through chorionic villous sampling or amniocentesis.
Pre-natal diagnosis allows for immediate postnatal nitisinone administration on the day of birth for known-affected infants, achieving the best possible outcomes.
In high-prevalence populations such as the Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean region of Quebec, population-wide carrier screening is offered and has substantially reduced disease burden.
For families with a known FAH mutation, pre-implantation genetic testing during IVF can ensure unaffected embryos are selected.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to the low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet and take the prescribed metabolic formula daily; dietary lapses raise plasma tyrosine and risk corneal or neurological complications.
Take nitisinone at the same time each day, since consistent dosing ensures continuous pathway blockade; missed doses allow succinylacetone to reappear.
Attend all scheduled monitoring visits — typically every three months in childhood — for plasma amino acids, succinylacetone, AFP, liver function, and growth assessment.
Keep a medical alert identification indicating the diagnosis and current medications, particularly important during emergency care when metabolic specialists are not immediately available.
Inform all treating clinicians, including dentists and surgeons, of the diagnosis before any procedure that involves fasting, anaesthesia, or protein catabolism.

recommended: Metabolic amino acid formula providing complete nutrition without tyrosine and phenylalanine — this is the primary protein source
Low-protein vegetables such as cucumber, lettuce, courgette, and most fruits that contain very small amounts of tyrosine
Specialised low-protein food products including low-protein pasta, bread, and rice available through metabolic dietitian prescription
Adequate energy from carbohydrate and fat sources to prevent protein catabolism that would release tyrosine from muscle
avoid: High-protein foods including meat, fish, chicken, eggs, dairy, legumes, and standard wheat products which contain substantial tyrosine and phenylalanine
Aspartame (an artificial sweetener metabolised to phenylalanine), present in diet soft drinks and many processed foods
Dietary practices such as intermittent fasting or very high protein intake that would destabilise amino acid balance

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity is encouraged and is not restricted by the diagnosis. Children on nitisinone and dietary management can participate in sport and physical education; adequate energy intake must be maintained to prevent protein catabolism during exercise. Patients with significant hepatic fibrosis or portal hypertension should have activity levels reviewed by the treating hepatologist, as contact sports and activities with high fall risk may need temporary modification.
dailyManagement: Take nitisinone at the prescribed dose and time daily; if a dose is missed, take it as soon as remembered the same day and inform the metabolic team.
Prepare and consume the daily metabolic formula in the prescribed volume; it is the principal source of protein and essential amino acids.
Measure and record food portions when natural protein is permitted to stay within the daily tyrosine and phenylalanine targets.
Report new or worsening symptoms — abdominal pain, jaundice, limb pain, or eye discomfort — promptly to the metabolic team rather than waiting for the next scheduled visit.
Carry emergency contact information for the metabolic centre at all times.
prognosis: The prognosis for type I tyrosinemia has been transformed by nitisinone. Patients diagnosed through newborn screening and started on nitisinone in the first week of life have a ten-year survival above 90%, near-normal liver function in most cases, and a residual hepatocellular carcinoma risk of approximately 10% compared with near-certainty without treatment. Growth and development are normal or near-normal when dietary control is good. Hepatocellular carcinoma risk, while dramatically reduced, is not eliminated even with optimal nitisinone therapy, and lifelong AFP and liver imaging surveillance is therefore mandatory. Patients who undergo liver transplantation have excellent outcomes at experienced centres with over 90% five-year survival. For type II, the prognosis is excellent with dietary management: corneal disease resolves without permanent visual impairment in most patients treated promptly, and skin lesions improve significantly. Intellectual development in type II is normal when corneal disease is managed and tyrosine is well controlled. Type III is very rare, and the long-term neurological prognosis is uncertain.
recoveryTimeline: In type I patients started on nitisinone from the newborn period, succinylacetone typically disappears from blood and urine within two to four weeks, coagulopathy improves within two to four weeks, and liver enzyme elevation resolves over one to three months. In patients presenting acutely with liver failure, clinical stabilisation with nitisinone may take two to six weeks. Renal tubular function improves gradually over three to twelve months. In type II, corneal crystals resolve within three to six weeks of starting the low-tyrosine diet, and skin lesions improve over three to six months.
complications: Hepatocellular carcinoma — the most feared complication of type I, affecting approximately 10% of patients even with optimal nitisinone therapy; the risk was nearly 100% in the pre-nitisinone era and correlates with age at which treatment began.

Neurological crises — acute episodes of porphyria-like pain, hypertonia, and paralysis from delta-aminolevulinic acid accumulation in type I; prevented by nitisinone but can occur during treatment lapses.

Renal tubular dysfunction and nephrocalcinosis — type I patients develop Fanconi syndrome with phosphate wasting and vitamin D resistance; usually reverses with nitisinone but may persist in those with delayed diagnosis.
Corneal scarring and visual impairment — occur in type II if corneal tyrosine crystal deposits go untreated; fully preventable with early dietary management.

Liver cirrhosis and portal hypertension — develop in type I patients with delayed diagnosis or poor treatment compliance; significantly increases surgical risk and the threshold for liver transplant listing.
Suboptimal neurocognitive outcomes — reported in some cohorts of type I patients despite adequate biochemical control, highlighting the importance of early treatment and educational support.

supportResources.name: National Organization for Rare Disorders (NORD) — Tyrosinemia Type I
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient-facing overview of type I tyrosinemia including diagnosis, treatment, and support resources.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Inherited Metabolic Disorders of the Liver
supportResources.description: Information on hereditary liver metabolic disorders, including tyrosinemia, from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: Metabolic Support UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, peer support, and advocacy for individuals and families affected by inherited metabolic disorders including tyrosinemia.

sources.title: Lindstedt S, Holme E, Lock EA et al. Treatment of hereditary tyrosinaemia type I by inhibition of 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase. Lancet, 1992
Russo PA, Mitchell GA, Tanguay RM. Tyrosinemia: a review. Pediatric and Developmental Pathology, 2001
van Ginkel WG, Rodenburg IL, Harding CO et al. Long-term outcomes and practical considerations in the pharmacological management of tyrosinemia type 1. Pediatric Drugs, 2016
Mayorandan S, Meyer U, Gokcay G et al. Cross-sectional study of 168 patients with hepatorenal tyrosinaemia and implications for clinical practice. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases, 2014
NICE — Nitisinone for treating hereditary tyrosinaemia type 1 (HTA 2019)

Larochelle J, Alvarez F, Bussieres JF et al. Effect of nitisinone (NTBC) treatment on the clinical and laboratory expression of hereditary tyrosinemia type 1: a review of 9 years experience. Canadian Journal of Gastroenterology, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Phenylketonuria (PKU)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PKU elevates phenylalanine (not tyrosine) due to phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency and causes intellectual disability without liver or kidney disease; tyrosinemia type I primarily damages the liver and kidneys.

confusedWithConditions.name: Galactosemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Galactosemia causes neonatal liver failure from lactose exposure after dairy feeding, with cataracts and E. coli sepsis; tyrosinemia type I presents with liver failure regardless of lactose intake and is identified by succinylacetone elevation, not reducing substances in urine.

confusedWithConditions.name: Wilson disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Wilson disease causes liver and neurological disease from copper accumulation, typically presenting in older children and young adults rather than infants; serum ceruloplasmin is low and Kayser-Fleischer rings are present, whereas succinylacetone is absent.

relatedConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are autosomal recessive disorders of aromatic amino acid metabolism diagnosed by newborn screening and managed with dietary restriction and, for PKU, pharmacological therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Maple syrup urine disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Another newborn-screen-detectable inborn error of amino acid catabolism causing acute neonatal metabolic crises and requiring lifelong dietary management.

relatedConditions.name: Homocystinuria
relatedConditions.relevance: An inborn error of sulfur-bearing amino acid metabolism sharing the same management philosophy of dietary amino acid restriction and specialist metabolic follow-up.

relatedConditions.name: Disorder of tyrosine metabolism (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader ICD category under which all three tyrosinemia types and other tyrosine pathway defects are classified.

faqs.question: What is tyrosinemia and how serious is it?
faqs.answer: Tyrosinemia is a rare inherited disorder preventing normal breakdown of the amino acid tyrosine. Type I, the most severe form, can cause acute liver failure in infancy and liver cancer in childhood if untreated. With modern treatment — a drug called nitisinone combined with a special diet — over 90% of patients survive to adulthood with good liver function.

faqs.question: How is tyrosinemia treated?
faqs.answer: Type I is treated with nitisinone (NTBC), a drug taken daily for life that blocks the formation of toxic metabolites, combined with a low-tyrosine and low-phenylalanine diet. Type II is managed with the same diet without nitisinone. Liver transplantation is reserved for acute liver failure or liver cancer not manageable by medical therapy.

faqs.question: Can tyrosinemia be detected before symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Yes. Newborn bloodspot screening detects succinylacetone — a marker unique to type I tyrosinemia — within the first few days of life, allowing nitisinone to begin before any liver damage occurs. Countries with expanded newborn screening programmes now diagnose most type I cases before symptoms develop.

faqs.question: What causes tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: Tyrosinemia is caused by mutations in genes encoding enzymes that break down tyrosine. Type I results from mutations in the FAH gene; type II from the TAT gene; type III from the HPD gene. All three forms are autosomal recessive, meaning a child must inherit one faulty copy from each parent to be affected.

faqs.question: Does tyrosinemia cause liver cancer?
faqs.answer: Type I tyrosinemia carries a significant hepatocellular carcinoma risk. Before nitisinone, nearly all type I patients developed liver cancer. With nitisinone started early in life, the risk falls to approximately 10% but is not eliminated, which is why patients undergo lifelong liver ultrasound or MRI and alpha-fetoprotein monitoring every 6–12 months.

faqs.question: Is tyrosinemia the same as phenylketonuria?
faqs.answer: No. Both are inherited amino acid disorders detected by newborn screening, but they affect different enzymes and cause different problems. PKU raises phenylalanine and causes intellectual disability without liver disease. Tyrosinemia type I raises a different set of toxic metabolites and primarily damages the liver and kidneys.

faqs.question: What does nitisinone do?
faqs.answer: Nitisinone inhibits an enzyme in the tyrosine breakdown pathway, stopping the body from producing the toxic metabolites that damage the liver and kidneys in type I tyrosinemia. It does not cure the underlying gene defect but prevents harm as long as it is taken. It raises plasma tyrosine, so a low-tyrosine diet is essential alongside it.

faqs.question: How common is tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: Type I affects approximately 1 in 100,000–120,000 people globally. It is significantly more common in French-Canadians from the Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean region (1 in 16,000) due to a founder mutation. Types II and III are considerably rarer.

faqs.question: What foods must a person with tyrosinemia avoid?
faqs.answer: People with tyrosinemia must avoid high-protein foods — meat, fish, eggs, dairy, legumes, and wheat products — that are rich in tyrosine and phenylalanine. A specialist metabolic formula provides protein without these amino acids. A metabolic dietitian prescribes the specific amounts of natural protein allowed daily.

faqs.question: Can tyrosinemia affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Type II tyrosinemia specifically causes painful corneal crystals (subepithelial tyrosine deposits) that produce photophobia, tearing, and red eyes. A low-tyrosine diet resolves these within 3–6 weeks. Type I and III do not typically cause primary eye disease.

faqs.question: Is tyrosinemia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. All three classical types are autosomal recessive: both parents must be carriers of a faulty gene copy for a child to be affected. Each pregnancy of two carriers has a 25% chance of producing an affected child. Genetic counselling and carrier testing are recommended for all family members.

faqs.question: What are neurological crises in tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: In type I, delta-aminolevulinic acid accumulates and can trigger sudden episodes of severe limb pain, vomiting, hypertonia, weakness, and in severe cases respiratory failure — resembling acute porphyria. These crises occur when nitisinone dosing lapses or before diagnosis. They are a medical emergency requiring hospital admission.

faqs.question: Can people with tyrosinemia live a normal life?
faqs.answer: Most patients diagnosed through newborn screening and started on nitisinone promptly lead largely normal lives. They need daily medication, a specialised low-protein diet, and regular monitoring, but they can attend school, work, exercise, and form relationships. The main ongoing concern is lifelong liver cancer surveillance.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: Liver transplantation is curative for the hepatic manifestations of type I, replacing the deficient enzyme in the liver. Nitisinone controls the disease but is not a cure — it must be taken lifelong. Gene therapy is under investigation and may offer a future curative option without transplantation.

faqs.question: How is tyrosinemia type II different from type I?
faqs.answer: Type I is the most severe form, causing liver failure and liver cancer risk; type II causes eye and skin disease (corneal crystals and palmoplantar thickening) from peripheral tyrosine deposition, without significant liver involvement. Type II is managed with diet alone, while type I requires nitisinone.

faqs.question: What is succinylacetone and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Succinylacetone is a metabolite produced only when the FAH enzyme is deficient in type I tyrosinemia. It is pathognomonic — finding it in blood or urine confirms the diagnosis. It also contributes to organ toxicity and its disappearance from blood and urine on nitisinone confirms effective treatment.

faqs.question: Why is alpha-fetoprotein monitored in tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a protein produced by liver cells that rises markedly in liver cancer. In type I tyrosinemia, AFP is often very high at diagnosis and typically falls with nitisinone treatment. A rising AFP despite treatment signals possible hepatocellular carcinoma and triggers urgent liver imaging.

faqs.question: Can a pregnant woman pass tyrosinemia to her baby?
faqs.answer: Parents who are both carriers of the same FAH gene mutation have a 25% chance in each pregnancy of having an affected child. Pre-natal diagnosis by chorionic villous sampling or amniocentesis is available once the family mutations are known. Pre-implantation genetic testing during IVF allows selection of unaffected embryos.

faqs.question: Which countries have the highest rates of tyrosinemia?
faqs.answer: The Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean region of Quebec, Canada, has the world's highest rate at 1 in 16,000 live births, due to a founder mutation carried by approximately 1 in 22 people in that region. Slightly elevated rates are also found in parts of Scandinavia, Finland, and some Middle Eastern populations.

faqs.question: What happens if tyrosinemia type I is untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, type I tyrosinemia causes progressive liver failure, renal tubular dysfunction with rickets, recurrent neurological crises, and nearly universal development of hepatocellular carcinoma by early childhood. Before nitisinone, most affected children required liver transplantation or died in early life.

metaDescription: Tyrosinemia is a rare inherited metabolic disorder causing liver failure and liver cancer risk. Learn about nitisinone treatment, diet, and newborn screening.
